metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add stack_id to S2's wipChain schema (AC5) with its assignment rule ('Gerrit topic if set, else tip change-id') so the field is explicitly owned and populated by S2's selection phase before S3 reads it.
findings.finding: S3 uses stack_id as the canonical key for the needs_rebase marker ('keyed by change_id + patchset_SHA' then 'stack_id upsert key' — the text uses both), and says 'stack_id = the Gerrit topic the auto-lander assigns to a stack at selection time.' However, S2's wipChain schema definition (AC5) lists {change_id, chain_member_ids[], tested_shas, verified_at, phase, re_drive_count} with no stack_id field. The entity that assigns stack_id (S2 at selection time) does not include it in its own schema.
findings.location: child 0525-6d71-8969-4512 (S3) — Outcome record section; child 0ee8-08de-bf0f-45ec (S2) — AC5 wipChain schema
findings.suggested_fix: In S2 AC5, extend the wipChain schema to: '{change_id, chain_member_ids[], stack_id, tested_shas{change_id→sha}, verified_at{change_id→ts}, phase, re_drive_count}' with the note: 'stack_id is assigned at selection time as the Gerrit topic (or tip change_id if no topic is set); this is the shared key S3 uses to key its needs_rebase marker.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a decision rule to the Outcome Provenance section specifying which LLM-Review -1 tag kinds map to `review_failed` (agent-remediable) vs. a distinct infra-veto outcome or `ci_failed`, co-located with the outcome enum definition.
findings.finding: The plan says `review_failed` is 'derived from … an `LLM-Review -1` by the land command (S4)', but the existing codebase defines two structurally distinct LLM-Review -1 kinds: `[LLM-Review: BLOCK — finding]` (agent must fix code) and `[LLM-Review: BLOCK — coverage-gap (...)]` (infra veto — not the agent's fault, maintainer re-triggers). The plan gives no decision rule for which -1 kind maps to `review_failed` vs. a different outcome, leaving the agent with an ambiguous and potentially wrong remediation signal.
findings.location: Context § Outcome provenance ('review_failed … derived from … an LLM-Review -1'); AC § Agent landing contract
findings.suggested_fix: Specify: `review_failed` = LLM-Review -1 tagged `[LLM-Review: BLOCK — finding]`; a coverage-gap -1 (`[LLM-Review: BLOCK — coverage-gap (...)]`) is treated as a transient infra failure and either retried or surfaced as a distinct `infra_error` outcome so the agent does not attempt to fix code.

findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback / back-out plan to the implementation spec: (a) a `git revert` + `git push refs/meta/config` recipe for the label config, and (b) a `docker compose down` / feature-flag / Autosubmit-label disable procedure for the bot service if it must be taken out of service post-deploy.
findings.finding: The plan modifies `infra/gerrit/project.config` (pushed to `refs/meta/config`, a live Gerrit ACL/config channel) and deploys a new always-on bot service, but specifies no explicit rollback or back-out procedure if either is found to be buggy or unsafe post-deployment.
findings.location: Phase 1 AC — Serial auto-lander service bullet; Security-invariants artifact bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback' subsection to the AC or ADR-0042: 'To revert the label: `git revert <sha> && git push origin HEAD:refs/meta/config`. To disable the bot without label removal: set LANDER_ENABLED=false env var (bot goes dormant, Autosubmit votes are ignored, agents fall back to manual submit).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the comment policy for the 409 conflict hand-back in S3 AC — either 'post a Gerrit comment naming the conflict (consistent with S2 TOCTOU comment)' or 'post NO comment (rely on the 409 HTTP error in bot logs)' — and align with S2's comment for TOCTOU exhaustion.
findings.finding: The Gerrit comment policy for the 409 rebase-conflict hand-back path is unspecified in S3, creating an owned-by-none gap. S2's N=5 TOCTOU exhaustion explicitly posts a Gerrit comment naming the reason; S3's CI-fail explicitly posts NO comment; S3's 409-conflict hand-back says only 'remove Autosubmit from all changes in the stack' with no mention of a comment. The agent receives needs_rebase but has no in-Gerrit explanation of why the conflict occurred or how to resolve it.
findings.location: child 0525-6d71-8969-4512 (S3) — AC: 'Rebase-conflict: on POST /rebase 409 ...' and 'CI-fail: ... post NO bot comment'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to S3's rebase-conflict AC: 'Post a Gerrit comment on the tip change naming the conflict reason ("rebase conflict — rebase onto current main and re-apply Autosubmit") — consistent with S2\'s TOCTOU comment, giving the agent an in-Gerrit explanation without requiring them to consult bot logs.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Disambiguate '(S3)' and '(S4)' in the Outcome provenance paragraph — either spell out 'story S3' / 'story S4' or replace with the story name to eliminate the AWS-S3 false-read.
findings.finding: The notation '(S3)' and '(S4)' in the Context section's 'Outcome provenance' paragraph is ambiguous — it reads as either AWS S3 storage or story-number references, which could mislead an executor about the state-persistence mechanism.
findings.location: Context section — 'Outcome provenance' paragraph: 'the bot *records* `needs_rebase`/`ci_failed` in its own state (S3)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'in its own state (S3)' → 'in its own state (story S3 — the state-dir/outcome-record)' and '(S4)' → '(story S4 — the land command)' to make the story-reference intent unambiguous.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify S4 AC4 to specify 'the auto-lander\'s recorded outcome is read exclusively via S5\'s read-only status endpoint (not direct state-dir access, which is container-internal); the status endpoint exposes the needs_rebase marker for the queried change_id.'
findings.finding: S4 AC4 reads the S3-recorded needs_rebase outcome via 'a read of its state dir / a small status endpoint' — a loose OR. In the deployed topology, land.py runs outside the autolander container (it is a project CLI run by agents), making direct state-dir access architecturally impossible. The ambiguity may lead implementers to attempt direct state-dir reads that cannot work in production, or to ship land.py with an unreachable fallback code path.
findings.location: child 73f5-4df7-4fac-4738 (S4) — AC4 '--wait termination/timeout'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'a read of its state dir / a small status endpoint' with 'the S5 read-only status endpoint (which exposes the S3 needs_rebase marker for the queried change_id; direct state-dir access is unavailable to external callers)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Retitle the epic (or add a subtitle) to reflect committed scope only — e.g., 'Parallel-agent auto-lander for main — Phase 1: serial lander (project infrastructure)' — so the title matches the body's committed deliverables without implying batching is in scope.
findings.finding: The epic title says 'serial->batching' implying batching is part of this epic's arc or deliverable, but the body commits ONLY to serial work — cross-stack batching was rejected and all throughput work is explicitly deferred to a separate idea. A reader (or agent) scanning the title will expect batching deliverables here and may either look for them or misclassify the epic's scope.
findings.location: Epic title: 'Parallel-agent auto-lander for main — serial->batching (project infrastructure)' vs. Phase 2 AC: 'No Phase-2 work is in this epic's committed scope.'
findings.suggested_fix: Rename to: 'Parallel-agent auto-lander for main — serial lander, Phase 1 (project infrastructure)' and note in the title or subtitle that Phase 2 (batching/bounded speculation) is tracked under idea `c3a9-5f4c-bdc6-4e27`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove or replace all S9 references in S3's outcome record section; replace 'S2/S3/S4/S9' with 'S2/S3/S4' and note that batch failure handling is deferred to idea c3a9-5f4c-bdc6-4e27.
findings.finding: S3's outcome record section references 'S9 (batch failure)' as a consumer of the needs_rebase marker cache in two places. S9 is an archived story not present in the sibling roster. These are dangling references in the authoritative spec of a shipped story.
findings.location: child 0525-6d71-8969-4512 (S3) — Outcome record section
findings.suggested_fix: In S3 Outcome record, change 'S4 (land command) and S9 (batch failure) consume this cache' to 'S4 (land command) consumes this cache' and change the shared key reference to 'S2/S3/S4'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the Verified-await timeout (e.g. 'wait up to N minutes; if Verified does not arrive, emit `ci_failed` and remove Autosubmit') and the `--wait` command's maximum blocking duration in the AC or a named constant.
findings.finding: No timeout or maximum wait duration is specified for the lander's 'await a fresh Verified +1' step, leaving the ci_failed transition condition unbounded and unimplementable without a design choice the executor must make.
findings.location: Phase 1 AC — 'Serial auto-lander service' bullet: 'awaits a fresh `Verified +1`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'awaits a fresh Verified +1' AC bullet: 'with a configurable timeout (default: 60 min, matching the GHA job timeout); on expiry, remove `Autosubmit` and return `ci_failed`.' Add to the `--wait` variant: 'blocks until an outcome is available or the same 60-min ceiling, then exits with `ci_failed`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `wipChain` in the plan (or in a Terminology section): what it contains (e.g. ordered list of change-ids currently being processed), its persistence model (in-memory vs state-dir file), and its lifecycle (created on Autosubmit detect, destroyed on terminal outcome).
findings.finding: The term `wipChain` is used without definition — an executor cannot implement it without inferring what data structure, lifecycle, or locking semantic it represents.
findings.location: Phase 1 AC — 'Serial auto-lander service' bullet: 'Single `wipChain` (serialized, one instance)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Terminology: '**wipChain**: the single in-progress chain the lander is currently processing — an ordered list of Gerrit change-ids persisted to the state dir; at most one exists at a time (serialized); cleared on terminal outcome (merged or failure handback).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the Autosubmit-label ACL spec with the failure-handling spec: either (a) explicitly state the bot account needs a separate 'remove Autosubmit' ACL grant (and narrow it in the security-invariants section), or (b) clarify that 'remove Autosubmit' is performed by the agent CLI on the bot's behalf — one or the other, not left implicit.
findings.finding: The Autosubmit-label AC states 'the bot casts no votes; the grant is tightly scoped, not a bot-only or open grant,' but the Failure-handling AC requires 'CI-fail → remove `Autosubmit`' — removing a label IS casting a vote (setting the value to 0/-1). These two ACs directly contradict each other: if the bot cannot cast votes it cannot remove the Autosubmit label, yet removing it is a required failure-handling step.
findings.location: AC Phase 1 — Autosubmit label bullet ('the bot casts no votes') vs Failure handling bullet ('CI-fail → remove `Autosubmit`')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to the Autosubmit label AC: 'The bot service account is granted a separate narrow ACL to remove (set to 0) the Autosubmit label on failure; this is distinct from the Contributors grant that allows setting +1.' Then add this ACL grant to the security-invariants enumeration in ADR-0042.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the 'single source of truth' language: either (a) restate it as 'the land command is the single interface for outcomes, sourcing `needs_rebase`/`ci_failed` from the bot's state store and `merged`/`review_failed` from Gerrit,' or (b) make the bot's state store the authoritative record for ALL four outcomes (bot writes `merged`/`review_failed` too, CLI reads only the store).
findings.finding: The phrase 'the bot is the single source of truth for the outcome' appears in both the Context ('Agent contract' paragraph) and the AC ('agent landing contract' bullet), but the Context's own Outcome-provenance note states that `merged` and `review_failed` are *derived by the land command (S4) from native Gerrit status*, not from the bot's state store. These two statements contradict each other: the bot cannot simultaneously be the single source of truth AND have two of its four outcomes sourced by the CLI from Gerrit.
findings.location: Context — 'Agent contract = one command → one typed outcome' paragraph and Outcome provenance note; AC Phase 1 — 'Agent landing contract' bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'the bot is the single source of truth for the outcome' with 'the land command is the single source of truth for the agent — it synthesises the outcome from the bot's state store (`needs_rebase`, `ci_failed`) and from Gerrit native status (`merged`, `review_failed`).' Mirror this wording in the AC bullet.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove the 'No Gerrit-specific landing logic in `src/rebar` core' Success Criterion bullet (or demote it to a note) and keep only the Out-of-scope entry, which already explains the deferral rationale — or vice versa, keeping only the Success Criterion and merging the deferral rationale into it.
findings.finding: 'No Gerrit-specific landing logic in `src/rebar` core' is stated as a Success Criterion AND also appears verbatim as an Out-of-scope item, making it a duplicate requirement across two sections with no added information in either instance.
findings.location: Success Criteria (last bullet) and Explicitly OUT of scope (second bullet)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand the doc-update AC to include a grep/audit step (e.g., `grep -r 'rebase.*retry\|manually.*rebase\|rebase to tip' docs/ *.md`) that identifies every other file referencing the old manual-landing workflow, and list each additional file requiring update or a forward-pointer to ADR-0042.
findings.finding: The doc-update scope is limited to `CLAUDE.md` and `CONTRIBUTING.md`; the plan does not call for a broader audit of other docs that may reference the old manual-rebase-and-retry landing workflow, risking stranded stale references elsewhere in the repo.
findings.location: Phase 1 AC — Docs bullet: 'rewrite this repo's landing section (CLAUDE.md + CONTRIBUTING.md)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a targeted spike/probe (or RED integration fixture) that calls `POST /changes/{id}/rebase` with `on_behalf_of_uploader` against the actual Gerrit instance and asserts that author identity, DCO `Signed-off-by`, and `rebar-ticket` trailers are preserved on the resulting patchset before committing the full autolander build-out.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that using `on_behalf_of_uploader` during `POST /changes/{id}/rebase` preserves author identity, DCO sign-offs, and `rebar-ticket` trailers, but no probe or spike is planned to verify this Gerrit API behavior before full build-out. Gerrit's on_behalf_of semantics for rebase (vs. plain rebase) are a specific integration point that can silently drop or alter commit metadata depending on server configuration, plugin version, and the rebase strategy used.
findings.location: Phase 1 AC — Serial auto-lander service bullet: '`on_behalf_of_uploader` to preserve author/DCO/`rebar-ticket` trailers'
findings.suggested_fix: Create a one-off spike story (or add to S1/the lead story's Definition of Done) that performs a test rebase on a scratch change with a known trailer set, retrieves the rebased patchset via `GET /changes/{id}/revisions/{rev}/commit`, and asserts all expected trailers are present. If on_behalf_of_uploader does not preserve trailers, the plan must specify an alternative (e.g., post-rebase amend, or a different Gerrit rebase option).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that S2 and S3 are treated as an indivisible deploy unit (shipped together or behind a feature flag), or explicitly merge their scope into one story, given that S2 without S3 cannot satisfy the 'never partial-land' invariant and is not safely releasable independently.
findings.finding: S2 (core loop happy path) and S3 (failure handling / stack-atomic hand-back) together form a single atomic behavior — the 'safe lander loop' — and S2 alone is not safely deployable: a lander with no conflict/CI-fail handling could hang or leave stacks in an indeterminate state. The decomposition splits these by execution path (happy vs. error), which is structurally analogous to a horizontal layer split, an anti-pattern per G5.
findings.location: Child stories S2 (perovskite-frilly-grebe) and S3 (darksome-starry-sable)
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) merge S2+S3 into one story 'Serial auto-lander core loop + failure handling' (the natural vertical slice of a safe lander), or (b) add an explicit note in S2's ACs that it ships only as part of a chain with S3 and is never deployed alone — making the dependency explicit rather than implicit.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `wipChain` selection policy for the multi-stack case (e.g. FIFO by `Autosubmit`-set timestamp, or earliest-`is:submittable`-ready-first) so the bot's queuing behavior is deterministic and starvation-free.
findings.finding: The plan mandates a single `wipChain` serializer but never specifies the policy for selecting WHICH stack to process next when multiple stacks concurrently hold the `Autosubmit` label. Without a stated selection policy (e.g. FIFO by label-set timestamp, or earliest-Verified-ready-first), implementations may choose arbitrary orderings that cause starvation (a large stack repeatedly re-queued while short stacks land) or livelock (two stacks alternately failing rebase), and the bot's behavior is undefined in the multi-stack case.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Phase 1, serial auto-lander service bullet: 'Single `wipChain` (serialized, one instance)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the autolander service AC: 'Selection policy: FIFO by `Autosubmit` label-set timestamp (earliest-set stack is processed first); ties broken by change number ascending.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the re-verify mechanism to explicitly require comparing the current patchset revision (from `GET /changes/{id}?o=CURRENT_REVISION`) against the Verified-voted patchset SHA before calling submit, not merely checking `is:submittable` on the change.
findings.finding: The plan's re-verify step ('re-verify `is:submittable` on the exact tested SHA immediately before submit') is ambiguous about HOW the bot confirms patchset identity: `is:submittable` checks whether the current head patchset of the change is submittable, but it does not confirm that the current patchset IS the same patchset that received `Verified +1`. If a new patchset is uploaded between the bot's Verified-await and its submit call (e.g. a manual amend by the owner), `is:submittable` will be false (Verified dropped by the new patchset's TRIVIAL_REBASE kind), so the bot correctly aborts — but the plan never states this check or the comparison. Without an explicit patchset-rev comparison (current patchset SHA == Verified-voted SHA), the re-verify mechanism is underspecified and an implementer may only call `is:submittable` without confirming patchset identity, leaving a narrow window where a stale patchset check passes.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Security-invariants artifact bullet: 're-verify `is:submittable` on the exact tested SHA immediately before submit'
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite the AC bullet to: 're-verify immediately before submit: (a) fetch the change's current patchset SHA via GET /changes/{id}?o=CURRENT_REVISION, (b) assert it equals the Verified-voted SHA the bot awaited, (c) assert is:submittable; abort if any check fails.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief 'Alternatives considered' note explaining why Gerrit's native `autosubmit` plugin was evaluated and rejected (or confirm it was not applicable, e.g., plugin doesn't enforce Verified re-run on rebase, ACL model mismatch, or deployment constraints) — cite the specific gap that makes the bespoke lander necessary.
findings.finding: The plan designs a bespoke `Autosubmit` label + serial lander bot without acknowledging Gerrit's own first-party 'autosubmit' plugin (gerrit.googlesource.com/plugins/autosubmit), which ships with Gerrit ≥3.x and implements the same opt-in-label → rebase → await-CI → submit pattern. This is load-bearing prior art the plan does not consider or reject.
findings.location: Context § 'Design settled in the brainstorm'; Phase 1 AC 'Autosubmit Gerrit label'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Context or Out-of-scope section, add: 'Gerrit's native `autosubmit` plugin (gerrit.googlesource.com/plugins/autosubmit) was evaluated and rejected because [specific reason — e.g., it submits as soon as labels are satisfied without the FFO-tip-confirmed re-verify step required by R5 / CVE-2025-1568 posture; or it is not bundled in 3.14.1 and requires a plugin load with Docker rebuild; or its ACL model doesn't support requester-votable with the tightly-scoped Contributors grant]. The bespoke lander is therefore necessary.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Surface `pygerrit2` (or a justified alternative) and `willfarrell/autoheal` as named dependencies in the plan text, with a one-line capability assertion per dependency confirming it supports the required operation (e.g., `rebase_on_behalf_of_uploader` field, Gerrit 3.14 REST compatibility for pygerrit2; Docker health-event watching without write access for autoheal).
findings.finding: The plan's implementation relies on `pygerrit2` as the Gerrit REST client (resolved as decision D5 in ticket comments) and `willfarrell/autoheal` as the hang-recovery sidecar (D4), but neither appears in the plan text. Both are unverified capability/maintenance assertions: `pygerrit2` has had no PyPI release since ~2018 and may lack support for `rebase_on_behalf_of_uploader` or modern Gerrit 3.14 REST semantics; `autoheal` is an unmaintained third-party image that mounts the Docker socket (a significant attack surface).
findings.location: Phase 1 AC 'Serial auto-lander service'; 'infra/compose/Dockerfile.*'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the autolander S3 state store as a Terraform-managed resource (or an explicitly named prefix in an existing bucket), add a scoped `s3:GetObject/PutObject/DeleteObject` inline policy to the EC2 instance role (following the iam_sN.tf pattern), and document it in the AC or a named `iam_autolander.tf` anchor file.
findings.finding: The plan states the autolander bot 'records needs_rebase/ci_failed in its own state (S3)' but never specifies: (a) whether this S3 bucket is an existing bucket or a new Terraform-managed aws_s3_bucket resource with versioning/encryption; (b) what IAM inline policy (scoped s3:GetObject/PutObject on the state bucket/prefix) is attached to the existing EC2 instance role to grant the autolander container access — the iam_sN.tf pattern used by all prior stories requires this; (c) whether plan-before-apply discipline covers this resource. The bot's persistent state store is unprovisioned infrastructure with no IAM grant specified.
findings.location: Context section — 'the bot *records* `needs_rebase`/`ci_failed` in its own state (S3); `merged`/`review_failed` are *derived* from native Gerrit status'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an `iam_autolander.tf` documenting: 'S3 state for the autolander is at `s3://rebar-<bucket>/autolander/` (resource declared in `autolander.tf`); access is granted via inline policy `rebar-gerrit-autolander-s3` on the shared instance role scoped to `arn:aws:s3:::rebar-<bucket>/autolander/*`.' Mirror the iam_s2.tf / iam_s4a.tf documentation-anchor pattern — even if the autolander reuses an existing bucket, the policy attachment must be explicit.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the SSM SecureString slot for the autolander's Gerrit credential in the plan (either a new `/rebar/prod/autolander-bot-token` entry in `ssm.tf`'s `rebar_secret_params`, or an explicit statement that `gerrit-bot-token` is shared with justification), and confirm whether the existing Gerrit `Service Users` ACL satisfies `on_behalf_of_uploader` or requires a new capability grant.
findings.finding: The auto-lander calls Gerrit REST APIs (POST /changes/{id}/rebase, /rebase:chain, submit) with `on_behalf_of_uploader`, which requires a Gerrit service-account HTTP credential (token or password). The plan does not name the SSM SecureString slot that holds this credential (e.g. `/rebar/prod/autolander-bot-token`), does not clarify whether it reuses the existing `gerrit-bot-token` slot, and does not state whether a new Gerrit service account is provisioned or the existing bot identity is reused. Without a named secret slot, the operator provisioning handoff is underspecified.
findings.location: Phase 1 AC — 'Serial auto-lander service: rebases to the current tip (POST /changes/{id}/rebase / /rebase:chain, on_behalf_of_uploader to preserve author/DCO/rebar-ticket trailers)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `monitoring_autolander.tf` (or equivalent section) to the plan's AC specifying a CloudWatch alarm for autolander error markers (e.g. `autolander_errors` metric published by the host observability probe), consistent with the per-story monitoring pattern established by monitoring_s4b.tf, monitoring_1fa8.tf, and monitoring_autodeploy.tf.
findings.finding: The plan specifies observability for the autolander as 'container stdout → existing CloudWatch monitoring' but does not specify a new per-service CloudWatch alarm for autolander failures. Every other service introduced by prior stories has a dedicated alarm (voter_errors for the review-bot, g2p_dispatch_errors for CI dispatch, deploy_errors for autodeploy); a stuck or error-looping autolander would leave the landing queue silently blocked with no operator alert.
findings.location: Phase 1 AC — 'Observability: the bot logs to its own service logs (container stdout → existing CloudWatch monitoring), NOT session_log'
findings.suggested_fix: Follow the monitoring_autodeploy.tf pattern: have the autolander write a structured AUTOLANDER_ERROR marker to stderr on each failure; the host observability probe greps the container's journald and publishes `rebar/host:autolander_errors`; a new CloudWatch alarm fires when the count > 0 in any 5-minute window and routes to the existing `aws_sns_topic.alerts`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and correct the chain-rebase endpoint notation: replace '/rebase:chain' with the Gerrit 3.x REST spec (`POST /changes/{id}/rebase` with body `{"rebase_chain": true}`) and confirm this exists in Gerrit 3.14.1 before committing the implementation story.
findings.finding: The plan cites 'POST /changes/{id}/rebase / /rebase:chain' as the chain-rebase endpoint, but '/rebase:chain' is not a valid Gerrit REST path. In Gerrit 3.x the chain-rebase capability is POST /changes/{id}/rebase with body field 'rebase_chain: true' (introduced in Gerrit 3.5+). The ':chain' suffix notation does not exist in the Gerrit REST API and, if implemented literally, will 404 in production.
findings.location: Phase 1 AC — Serial auto-lander service bullet: 'POST /changes/{id}/rebase / `/rebase:chain`'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the plan text '/rebase:chain' with 'POST /changes/{id}/rebase body={"rebase_chain": true}' (the documented Gerrit RebaseInput field). Add a note confirming this is supported in Gerrit 3.14.1.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a targeted integration probe (or a RED e2e fixture in the autolander story) that calls `POST /changes/{id}/rebase` with `on_behalf_of_uploader=true` on the actual Gerrit 3.14.1 instance and asserts: (a) the call succeeds (confirming the autolander account has the required global capability), and (b) the rebased patchset retains its original author and all commit-message trailers (Signed-off-by, rebar-ticket).
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'on_behalf_of_uploader' in the rebase call preserves author/DCO/rebar-ticket trailers, but no probe or spike is named to verify this behavior on the live Gerrit 3.14.1 instance. 'on_behalf_of_uploader' in RebaseInput is a specific Gerrit capability that (a) requires the rebasing account to hold the 'Rebase on Behalf of Uploader' global capability and (b) has specific semantics around commit authorship that differ from a normal rebase — its trailer-preservation behavior is not guaranteed without a live test.
findings.location: Phase 1 AC — Serial auto-lander service bullet: '`on_behalf_of_uploader` to preserve author/DCO/`rebar-ticket` trailers'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state the S3 integration preconditions for the autolander's outcome store: name the new bucket (or confirm reuse of an existing one), the required IAM inline policy expansion on the EC2 instance role (s3:GetObject/PutObject scoped to the autolander prefix), and the Terraform resource story that provisions it before the autolander service deploys.
findings.finding: The plan states the bot 'records needs_rebase/ci_failed in its own state (S3)' as a new outcome-provenance store, but states no auth preconditions, IAM policy expansion, or S3 bucket provisioning plan for this integration. The existing infra has no autolander S3 bucket (the codebase uses SSM for secrets and EBS bind-mounts for service state); adding S3 requires IAM inline policy expansion on the EC2 instance role, a new bucket, and possibly new SSM parameters.
findings.location: Context section: 'Outcome provenance: the bot records needs_rebase/ci_failed in its own state (S3)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC or dependency note: 'Terraform provisions an autolander-state S3 bucket (or prefix in the existing tfstate bucket); the EC2 instance role gets a scoped s3:GetObject/PutObject inline policy; the bucket name is injected as an SSM parameter / env var into the autolander container.' Alternatively, replace S3 with an EBS bind-mount (matching the existing review-bot pattern) to eliminate the new AWS dependency entirely.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the autolander's Gerrit service identity: whether it is a new Service User or reuses rebar-review-bot; which groups it joins or which explicit ACL grants it receives (Submit on refs/heads/*, Rebase on Behalf of Uploader global capability, Autosubmit vote if the bot itself removes it on failure); and update project.config / setup-project.sh accordingly in the relevant story's AC.
findings.finding: The plan does not state the auth identity or ACL grants needed for the new autolander service. The autolander must: (a) vote/remove the Autosubmit label (requester-votable by Contributors — but is the bot in Contributors?), (b) call POST /changes/{id}/rebase on_behalf_of_uploader (requires the 'Rebase on Behalf of Uploader' global capability), and (c) call POST /changes/{id}/submit (requires Submit ACL, currently restricted to Contributors + Administrators). Whether the autolander reuses the review-bot's Service User identity or gets a new one, and what new ACL grants it requires, is unstated.
findings.location: Phase 1 AC — Autosubmit label bullet: 'requester-votable ACL (vote granted to group Contributors, so an agent sets the label under its OWN identity via the land command — the bot casts no votes)'; Serial auto-lander service bullet: 'ancestor-atomic submits the exact tested SHA'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the auto-lander's Gerrit identity and ACL grants in the plan: specify whether it reuses the existing bot identity or gets its own service account + SSM slot, add the required `rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader` ACL entry (project.config stanza or All-Projects grant), specify how the lander gets `submit` rights (added to Contributors, or a dedicated service-user submit grant), and add these grants to the ADR-0042 Security invariants enumeration with their concrete audit checks.
findings.finding: The auto-lander service's own Gerrit identity and authorization grants are undeclared. To call `POST /changes/{id}/rebase` with `on_behalf_of_uploader` the bot needs the Gerrit `rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader` permission (a distinct ACL entry in project.config or All-Projects since Gerrit 3.6). To submit the change, the lander must also be authorized to `submit` — yet the existing project.config restricts `submit` to `Contributors + Administrators` and grants it only to human-facing groups, not to Service Users. The plan does not declare: (a) whether the auto-lander uses the existing review-bot identity or a new one; (b) what new ACL entries are added to project.config for rebase-on-behalf-of and submit; (c) whether the SSM parameter set gains a new slot for the lander credential. These are new authorization grants on the internet-exposed Gerrit server and represent an undeclared access posture on a boundary-crossing path.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Phase 1, serial auto-lander bullet 2 (`rebase … on_behalf_of_uploader … ancestor-atomic submits`) and Security-invariants artifact bullet (ADR-0042 invariants enumeration)
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's Security invariants AC, add two invariants: (1) 'lander bot holds rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader permission — verified by grep of project.config (or All-Projects) for the lander group'; (2) 'lander submit grant is narrowly scoped to the lander's own service-user identity, not all Service Users — verified by GET /projects/rebar/access diff'. If the lander gets a separate identity, add `/rebar/prod/autolander-gerrit-token` to ssm.tf and fetch-secrets.sh. If it reuses the review-bot identity, declare that explicitly and justify the combined privilege as acceptable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the outcome-record schema (fields, types, and any versioning) inline in the epic plan or in a dedicated schema section, so the bot writer and the land-status reader share a concrete contract.
findings.finding: The 'outcome-record schema' is referenced by name in the Greenfield note as a new artifact, and the Outcome Provenance note says the bot 'records needs_rebase/ci_failed in its own state (S3)', but the schema's field vocabulary (fields, types, versioning) is never defined anywhere in this plan. An implementer of the land-status command — which must read and surface these records — cannot implement it without knowing the record shape.
findings.location: Context § Outcome provenance; Greenfield note ('the outcome-record schema … are all NEW artifacts')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Outcome record schema' subsection to the Context or AC with at minimum: `change_id` (Gerrit change number), `patchset_sha`, `outcome` (enum value), `recorded_at` (ISO-8601 timestamp), and `reason` (human-readable string). Reference this schema from both the bot AC and the land-status command AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a 409-disambiguation decision rule to the Failure handling AC specifying how the bot distinguishes a true merge conflict (content conflict → agent must resolve) from a submit-race/tip-advance 409 (bot should retry rebase, not hand back to agent).
findings.finding: The failure AC maps a Gerrit HTTP 409 response to 'record a conflict outcome', but Gerrit returns 409 for two distinct cases: (a) a true merge conflict during rebase, and (b) a submit-race where a concurrent change landed first and the change is no longer at tip. These require different agent actions (fix conflict vs. simply retry rebase-to-new-tip), yet the plan provides no decision rule for distinguishing them.
findings.location: AC § Phase 1 — Failure handling ('rebase-conflict (409) → remove Autosubmit and record a conflict outcome')
findings.suggested_fix: Parse the 409 response body: Gerrit's conflict response body contains 'merge conflict' text; a clean 409 on an already-up-to-date change does not. Map content-conflict 409 to `needs_rebase` with a conflict flag; map tip-advance 409 to an internal bot retry (transparent to agent).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the wipChain state structure (at minimum: change_id(s), current patchset SHA, phase, lock/owner token, started_at) and specify crash-recovery semantics (e.g. on restart, re-check Gerrit state before acting) in the serial auto-lander AC.
findings.finding: The plan names `wipChain` as the serialization mechanism for the serial lander ('Single `wipChain` (serialized, one instance)') but never defines its state fields, lifecycle (creation on Autosubmit-set, update on rebase/CI events, deletion on terminal outcome), or crash-recovery fallback — leaving an unspecified fallback for the incomplete-sub-step scenario where the service crashes mid-flight.
findings.location: AC § Phase 1 — Serial auto-lander service ('Single `wipChain` (serialized, one instance)')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the hand-back mechanism (remove Autosubmit + write needs_rebase marker) as a shared utility owned by S3 but forward-declared/stubbed in S2, and add S3 as an explicit implementation-coupling note in S2's Dependencies section.
findings.finding: S2's N=5 TOCTOU exhaustion explicitly reuses S3's needs_rebase hand-back mechanism, but S2's Dependencies section only declares S1 (not S3). Meanwhile S3 declares S2 as its only dependency. This creates an unresolvable circular dependency: S2 cannot be fully implemented without S3's hand-back mechanism, but S3 depends on S2 being complete first. The forward-reference is real implementation coupling, not merely a greenfield cross-reference.
findings.location: child 0ee8-08de-bf0f-45ec (S2) — Scope section and AC4 hand-back paragraph; child 0525-6d71-8969-4512 (S3) — Dependencies section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to S2's Dependencies: 'Implementation coupling: S2 invokes S3's hand-back utility for N=5 TOCTOU exhaustion; the hand-back function must be implemented as a shared module (e.g. infra/autolander/handback.py) that S3 delivers and S2 imports.' Update S3 accordingly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Designate a single owner for the ADR-0042 Security invariants section (recommend S6, as it authors the ADR) and add an explicit handoff from S1: S1 delivers the label-specific grep check and the re-verify test reference; S6 assembles all invariants (including S2's TOCTOU guard test citation) into the ADR-0042 Security invariants section.
findings.finding: The ADR-0042 Security invariants section is claimed by two children with no explicit handoff: S1 AC5 states 'the ADR-0042 Security invariants section records the non-gating label + bot re-verifies invariant ... with its concrete checks (a grep of infra/gerrit/project.config ... and/or a test asserting the re-verify logic)' — implying S1 delivers this section. S6 authors ADR-0042 as its primary deliverable. The concrete check for re-verify (the test) lives in S2. No child specifies how S1's section content is assembled into S6's ADR, leaving the invariants section as a partial-without-remainder: S1 specifies content, S2 implements the test, S6 writes the document, with no explicit integration step.
findings.location: child 843b-801f-9ef9-442d (S1) — AC5; child b825-a233-97d4-45c2 (S6) — overall scope; child 0ee8-08de-bf0f-45ec (S2) — Verification/tests
findings.suggested_fix: In S1 AC5, change 'the ADR-0042 Security invariants section records ...' to 'S1 delivers the following Security invariants inputs for S6 to assemble into ADR-0042: (a) grep of infra/gerrit/project.config proving function = NoBlock and no submit-requirement reference; (b) GET /projects/rebar/access diff confirming Contributors-only ACL.' In S6, add 'Assemble the ADR-0042 Security invariants section from S1\'s label checks, S2\'s TOCTOU guard test reference, and the two-vote gate invariant narrative.'

findings.finding: The plan writes `POST /changes/{id}/rebase` / `/rebase:chain` using a slash-separator notation that implies `/rebase:chain` is a distinct REST path. In Gerrit's REST API the chain-rebase variant is `POST /changes/{id}/rebase` with a `RebaseInput` JSON body containing `{"rebase_chain": true}` — there is no standalone `/rebase:chain` path. The colon-action path form exists in Gerrit (e.g. `/submit`, `/abandon`) but not for the chain-rebase surface.
findings.location: Phase 1 AC — Serial auto-lander service bullet: 'POST /changes/{id}/rebase / /rebase:chain'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'POST /changes/{id}/rebase / /rebase:chain' with 'POST /changes/{id}/rebase (body: {"rebase_chain": true} for a relation chain; RebaseInput.rebase_chain, available since Gerrit 3.5, confirmed present in 3.14.1)' and add a one-line smoke-test in the e2e harness that calls this endpoint on a two-change chain.

findings.finding: Parent AC1 states the Autosubmit label has a 'bot-only vote ACL (service account autolander-bot / group Autosubmit Voters)' but every child (S1, S2, S4) consistently implements a Contributors-votable ACL where agents set the label themselves. S1 grants 'label-Autosubmit = -1..+1 group Contributors'; S2 declares 'the vote is granted to group Contributors, so an agent sets the label'; S4 sets it 'under the agent's OWN Gerrit identity (a Contributor).' The parent's AC1 wording is internally contradicted by the parent's own body ('Autosubmit opt-in label = a lease the agent hands the bot') and by all children. Additionally, parent AC8 lists 'bot-only vote ACL' as a security invariant to enumerate in ADR-0042, so S1 AC5 would document that invariant using the wrong (bot-only) framing — producing a false security claim in the security-invariants artifact.
findings.location: child 843b-801f-9ef9-442d (S1) AC1 / AC5; parent Phase 1 AC1; parent AC8
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite parent AC1 vote-ACL clause to: 'vote ACL granted to Contributors (agents set the label under their own identity via the land command), with the bot holding only rebase-on-behalf-of and submit capabilities; the two-vote gate is enforced by the bot re-verifying is:submittable on the exact tested SHA.' Update S1 AC5 to replace 'bot-only vote ACL' with 'Contributors-votable with bot-only submit capability' in the invariant enumeration.

findings.finding: The ADR-0042 security invariants section content is split across two stories with no integration path: S1 AC5 authors the security invariants content ('the ADR-0042 Security invariants section records...') while S6 creates the ADR-0042 file ('docs/adr/0042-*.md records the auto-lander...') but S6 does NOT depend on S1 and does not mention integrating S1's security invariants content. Neither story assigns ownership of the final, merged ADR-0042 file that contains BOTH the design record (S6's scope) and the security invariants section (S1's scope).
findings.location: child 843b-801f-9ef9-442d (S1) AC5; child b825-a233-97d4-45c2 (S6) AC3; S6 Dependencies
findings.suggested_fix: Add to S6's dependencies: 'Also depends on S1 for the Security invariants section content.' Add to S6 AC3: 'The ADR includes a Security invariants section (content drafted by S1) enumerating each invariant per parent AC8.' Remove the ADR-0042 authorship claim from S1 AC5 and replace it with: 'Document the security invariants in a structured artifact (e.g. infra/gerrit/security-invariants.md or inline in the PR description) that S6 will incorporate into ADR-0042.'

findings.finding: The parent AC specifies a 'bot-only vote ACL (service account `autolander-bot` / group `Autosubmit Voters`)' for the Autosubmit label, but S1 contradicts this by granting the label ACL to group `Contributors` (requesters) so agents can set it under their own identity. These two specifications are irreconcilable as written.
findings.location: child 843b-801f-9ef9-442d: S1 — AC lines 'label-Autosubmit = -1..+1 group Contributors' vs parent Phase-1 AC bullet 1 'bot-only vote ACL (service account autolander-bot / group Autosubmit Voters)'
findings.suggested_fix: Update the parent AC bullet 1 to read: 'vote ACL granted to Contributors (requesters) so agents may set their own intent; the two-vote gate is not weakened because the bot re-verifies `is:submittable` on the exact tested SHA immediately before submit' — aligning with S1's design rationale and ADR-0042's security invariant argument.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
